course_info.title: Information Architecture
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

learning_objectives: Define information architecture and its role in UX design
Identify the four components of IA: organization, labeling, navigation, search
Analyze existing information architectures for strengths and weaknesses
readings: Information Architecture by Rosenfeld, Morville & Arango, Ch. 1-3
How to Make Sense of Any Mess by Abby Covert, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Information architecture is the structural design of shared information environments. It determines how users find, understand, and navigate content — the invisible scaffolding behind every digital experience.
key_concepts: The four systems of IA: organization, labeling, navigation, search
The information architecture ecosystem: users, content, context
Top-down vs. bottom-up information architecture
IA deliverables: site maps, taxonomies, controlled vocabularies
The cost of poor IA: support costs, abandonment, user frustration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of two e-commerce sites with contrasting IA approaches — mapping their navigation structures on the whiteboard
setup_requirements: Miro or FigJam for collaborative diagramming

review.value: Organization (how content is grouped), Labeling (what things are called), Navigation (how users move through content), Search (how users find specific items)
Effective IA balances three forces: user needs and behaviors, content volume and structure, and business context and goals

learning_objectives: Distinguish between exact and ambiguous organization schemes
Select appropriate organizational structures for different content types
Apply faceted classification to complex content collections
readings: Information Architecture by Rosenfeld, Morville & Arango, Ch. 5-6
Everyday Information Architecture by Lisa Maria Martin, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: How you organize content determines whether users can find it. Organization schemes and structures are the backbone of information architecture.
key_concepts: Exact schemes: alphabetical, chronological, geographical
Ambiguous schemes: topic, task, audience, metaphor
Hierarchical structures: broad/shallow vs. narrow/deep
Faceted classification: multiple simultaneous organization dimensions
Polyhierarchy: items that belong in multiple categories
lecture_notes.demonstration: Organizing a recipe collection using different schemes — by cuisine, by difficulty, by ingredient, by meal type — and discussing which serves different user needs
setup_requirements: Recipe card set for hands-on sorting exercise

review.value: Organizing content along multiple independent dimensions (facets) simultaneously — a shirt can be organized by color, size, brand, and price without forcing a single hierarchy
Broad (many items per level, few levels) reduces clicks but increases scanning; deep (few items per level, many levels) reduces scanning but increases clicks — balance based on content and user needs

learning_objectives: Create clear, consistent labels that match user language
Build controlled vocabularies to manage terminology across a product
Test label effectiveness through first-click testing and card sorting
readings: Information Architecture by Rosenfeld, Morville & Arango, Ch. 7
Content Strategy for the Web by Kristina Halvorson, Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Labeling is the most underestimated component of IA. A perfectly organized system fails if the labels do not match users' vocabulary and expectations.
key_concepts: Label types: headings, navigation links, index terms, icons as labels
The vocabulary problem: users and organizations use different words
Controlled vocabularies: synonyms, preferred terms, related terms
Label testing methods: card sorting, first-click tests, tree tests
Writing effective navigation labels: clear, consistent, mutually exclusive
lecture_notes.demonstration: Label audit of a university website — identifying inconsistencies, jargon, and ambiguity across navigation levels
setup_requirements: First-click testing tool (Optimal Workshop or UsabilityHub)

review.value: A standardized set of terms (with synonyms and relationships) that ensures consistent labeling across a product — 'Settings' not a mix of 'Settings,' 'Preferences,' 'Options,' and 'Configuration'
Research shows users and subject matter experts use different words for the same concepts — label testing with real users is essential, not just internal agreement

learning_objectives: Select and implement navigation patterns appropriate for content size and depth
Design wayfinding systems that orient users within deep hierarchies
Evaluate navigation effectiveness using tree testing and analytics
readings: Information Architecture by Rosenfeld, Morville & Arango, Ch. 8-9
Designing Web Navigation by James Kalbach, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Navigation is how users traverse your information architecture. The best IA in the world fails if the navigation system does not expose it effectively.
key_concepts: Global, local, contextual, and supplemental navigation
Breadcrumbs and their role in wayfinding
Mega menus vs. progressive disclosure for large content sets
Mobile navigation patterns: tab bar, hamburger, hub-and-spoke
Search as navigation: when users skip the hierarchy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing navigation for a 500-page content site using a combination of global, local, and faceted navigation in Figma

review.value: Global navigation provides consistent access to top-level sections from any page; local navigation shows options within the current section — both are needed for effective wayfinding
Showing only the navigation options relevant to the user's current context, revealing deeper options on demand — prevents overwhelming users with all options at once

learning_objectives: Create site maps that communicate IA decisions to stakeholders and developers
Model content types with attributes, relationships, and metadata schemas
Use content inventories and audits to inform IA decisions
readings: Everyday Information Architecture by Lisa Maria Martin, Ch. 4-5
Content Modeling for IA Practitioners (A List Apart article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Site maps and content models are the blueprints of information architecture. They translate abstract organizational decisions into concrete structures that development teams can build.
key_concepts: Visual site map notation: pages, page stacks, decision points, external links
Content models: content types, attributes, relationships
Metadata schemas: what information describes each content item
Content inventories: cataloging existing content for migration or redesign
Content audits: evaluating quality, accuracy, and relevance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a site map and content model for a museum website — from content inventory spreadsheet to visual site map in Figma

review.value: A structured definition of content types, their attributes, and relationships — e.g., an 'Event' has title, date, location, description, related exhibits, and speaker bios
A comprehensive spreadsheet cataloging every piece of content on a site — URL, title, content type, owner, last updated, quality rating — the essential starting point for IA redesigns

learning_objectives: Design search interfaces that support user search behavior patterns
Implement search features: autocomplete, filters, faceted search, sort options
Evaluate search effectiveness using search analytics and relevance metrics
readings: Search Patterns by Peter Morville & Jeffery Callender, Ch. 1-4
Designing the Search Experience by Tony Russell-Rose & Tyler Tate, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Search is both a navigation strategy and a distinct IA system. When browsing fails, search is the safety net. Designing effective search requires understanding how users formulate queries and evaluate results.
key_concepts: Search behavior: known-item search, exploratory search, refinding
Query formulation: natural language, keywords, filters
Search results: relevance ranking, snippets, metadata display
Faceted search: combining search with IA organization dimensions
Search analytics: top queries, zero-result queries, click-through rates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing search logs from a real e-commerce site — identifying failed queries, popular searches, and opportunities to improve the browsing IA based on search behavior

review.value: Searches that return no results — the most valuable signal in search analytics because they reveal content gaps, vocabulary mismatches, or user needs the product does not address
Combining keyword search with IA facets (filters) — users can search for 'running shoes' then narrow by size, color, price, and brand using the IA's organizational dimensions

questions.question: A large hospital system is redesigning its patient-facing website. The current site is organized by department (Cardiology, Orthopedics, Neurology, etc.). Many patients complain they do not know which department to visit for their symptoms. Propose an alternative organization scheme and explain why it better serves patient needs.
questions.answer: A task-based or symptom-based organization scheme would better serve patients. Instead of departmental silos, the IA should be organized around patient goals: 'I have a symptom,' 'I need to schedule an appointment,' 'I need to find a doctor,' 'I need to manage my health records.' For symptom-based browsing, faceted classification allows patients to search by symptom, condition, or body area, which maps to the correct department behind the scenes. This works because patients think in terms of their own health concerns, not hospital organizational structure.

rubric.key: Explains why departmental organization fails patients
Provides specific examples of the proposed scheme

questions.question: What is the difference between a taxonomy and a controlled vocabulary? When would you need each?
questions.answer: A taxonomy is a hierarchical classification system that organizes concepts into parent-child relationships (Animals > Mammals > Dogs > Labrador). A controlled vocabulary is a flat or structured list of authorized terms with synonym mappings (preferred term: 'Automobile,' synonyms: 'Car,' 'Vehicle,' 'Auto'). Use a taxonomy when content has natural hierarchical relationships and users need to browse from general to specific. Use a controlled vocabulary when content needs consistent labeling across a product, especially when multiple authors create content using different terminology for the same concept.

rubric.key: Correct definition of taxonomy with hierarchical emphasis
Correct definition of controlled vocabulary with synonym emphasis

questions.question: You are designing the IA for an online learning platform with 2,000+ courses across 50 subject areas. The current navigation uses a single dropdown menu listing all 50 subjects alphabetically. Propose a better navigation strategy and justify your choices.
questions.answer: Replace the single alphabetical dropdown with a multi-level navigation strategy: (1) A mega menu grouping the 50 subjects into 6-8 high-level categories (Technology, Business, Creative Arts, etc.) with the subjects listed under each category. (2) Faceted filtering on the course catalog page allowing users to narrow by subject, difficulty level, duration, and rating simultaneously. (3) Prominent search with autocomplete suggesting both courses and subjects. (4) Personalized 'recommended' navigation based on user history. This works because 50 items in a flat list exceeds working memory (Miller's 7 plus or minus 2), and different users have different mental models for categorizing subjects.

rubric.key: Identifies the problem with 50-item flat list
Includes faceted filtering or search component
Justifies choices with user behavior reasoning

questions.question: Define a content model for a 'Recipe' content type. List at least 6 attributes, specify which are required vs. optional, and identify one relationship to another content type.
questions.answer: Recipe content model — Required attributes: title (text, max 100 chars), ingredients (structured list with quantity, unit, ingredient name), instructions (ordered list of steps), prep_time (integer, minutes), cook_time (integer, minutes), servings (integer). Optional attributes: description (text), difficulty_level (enum: easy, medium, hard), cuisine_type (taxonomy term), dietary_tags (multi-select: vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free), nutrition_info (structured: calories, protein, fat, carbs), hero_image (media). Relationship: Recipe belongs to one or more Collection content types (e.g., 'Weeknight Dinners,' 'Holiday Favorites') — a many-to-many relationship.

rubric.key: At least 6 well-defined attributes with data types
Required vs. optional distinction is logical
Relationship to another content type is well-defined

major_assignments.title: Information Architecture Redesign
major_assignments.description: Select a real website with significant IA problems (at least 100 pages of content). Conduct a content inventory, perform card sorting research with at least 10 participants, design a new IA, and validate it with tree testing.
objectives: Conduct a content inventory and audit of an existing site
Use card sorting to understand user mental models for the content
Design a complete IA with site map, navigation, and labeling system
Validate the new IA through tree testing
deliverables: Content inventory spreadsheet with audit annotations
Card sorting study report with dendrogram analysis (minimum 10 participants)
New site map (visual, created in Figma or OmniGraffle)
Navigation specification document with labeling system
Tree test results showing findability improvements (minimum 15 participants)

criteria: Comprehensive catalog of existing content
Audit identifies content gaps, redundancy, and quality issues
Clear documentation of content types and relationships
Actionable recommendations for content (keep, revise, remove)
Card sort properly designed and conducted with sufficient participants
Analysis reveals clear patterns with dendrogram interpretation
Findings translated into concrete IA recommendations
Research methodology is reproducible and well-documented
Site map is comprehensive, clear, and well-organized
Navigation system matches content scale and user needs
Labels are clear, consistent, and user-tested
Content model supports all identified content types
Tree test designed with realistic tasks covering key user goals
Sufficient participants for meaningful results
Results demonstrate findability improvement over original
Interpretation addresses both successes and remaining issues
Thorough content inventory with insightful audit
Well-executed card sorting research with clear findings
Complete and logical IA design with site map and navigation
Tree test validation showing measurable improvement

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on IA theory, organization schemes, and navigation patterns

ia_exercises.description: Weekly exercises on content inventories, site mapping, labeling, and navigation design

ia_redesign_project.description: End-to-end IA redesign of a real website with research and validation

peer_ia_review.description: Structured peer review of classmates' IA redesign projects using evaluation criteria

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam on IA theory, methods, and practical application

course_policies.attendance: Attendance expected for all sessions. Card sorting and tree testing exercises require in-class participation that cannot be replicated. More than 3 absences reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Content inventory and research phases have hard deadlines that gate subsequent work. Late submissions lose 10% per day and may delay the overall project timeline.
course_policies.collaboration: Research studies may use shared participant pools. All analysis, design, and documentation must be individual work. Card sort data must not be shared between students.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research must use real participants. Content inventories must be original work. Cite all sources including IA patterns and design system references.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma or OmniGraffle for site maps, Optimal Workshop for card sorting and tree testing, spreadsheet software for content inventories, and Miro for collaborative exercises.
